choices.a: Graph showing frequency starting at about 1040 Hz and decreasing linearly
choices.b: Graph showing frequency starting at about 920 Hz and increasing linearly to about 1080 Hz
choices.c: Graph showing frequency constant at about 1000 Hz with sharp drop to about 920 Hz around time 3
choices.d: Graph showing frequency starting at about 1080 Hz, decreasing gradually, then dropping sharply to about 920 Hz

questions.diagram_description: Four frequency vs time graphs labeled a), b), c), d) showing different patterns for ambulance siren Doppler effect

concepts: wave frequency shifts due to relative motion
steps: Step 1: Identify the Doppler effect scenario - observer stationary, source moving
Step 2: As ambulance approaches, source velocity has component toward observer, so observed frequency f' > f₀ = 1000 Hz
Step 3: As ambulance recedes, source velocity has component away from observer, so observed frequency f' < f₀ = 1000 Hz
Step 4: The transition from higher to lower frequency occurs when the ambulance passes the observer
Step 5: Graph d) shows initial high frequency (~1080 Hz) when approaching, gradual decrease as it gets closer, then sharp drop to low frequency (~920 Hz) when it passes and recedes
solution.explanation: The Doppler effect causes higher frequency when the ambulance approaches and lower frequency when it recedes, with a sharp transition at the moment it passes the observer.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Which of the following radiation types has the longest wavelength?

choices.e: They all have at the same wavelength

steps: Step 1: Recall the electromagnetic spectrum in order of increasing wavelength (decreasing frequency)
Step 2: The order from shortest to longest wavelength is: gamma rays, X-rays, ultraviolet, visible light, infrared, microwaves, radio waves
Step 3: From the given choices, identify where each falls on the spectrum: X-rays (shortest), visible light, infrared light, radio waves (longest)
Step 4: Radio waves have wavelengths ranging from meters to kilometers, which is much longer than all other options listed
solution.explanation: Radio waves have the longest wavelengths in the electromagnetic spectrum, ranging from meters to kilometers, while X-rays have the shortest wavelengths among the choices given.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A cup filled with water has a hole in the side, through which the liquid is flowing out. If the cup is dropped from a height, what will happen to the water flowing from the cup?

choices.a: It will keep coming out, flowing at the same rate as before:
choices.b: It will keep coming out, but it will flow a bit slower than before.
choices.c: It will keep coming out, but start to flow upwards relative to the cup:
choices.d: It will keep coming out, flowing horizontally with respect to the falling cup:

questions.diagram_description: Five diagrams showing a cup with water and hole at different orientations during free fall

steps: Step 1: When the cup is at rest, water flows out due to hydrostatic pressure according to Torricelli's law
Step 2: When the cup is dropped, both the cup and water experience the same acceleration g downward
Step 3: In the reference frame of the falling cup, there is no net force causing the water to flow out
Step 4: The hydrostatic pressure that normally drives the flow is eliminated because both the cup and water are in free fall
Step 5: Without pressure difference, the water stops flowing out of the hole
solution.explanation: In free fall, both the cup and water experience weightlessness. The hydrostatic pressure that normally drives water flow is eliminated, so the water stops flowing out of the hole.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two cubes, with respective masses $M_1$ and $M_2$ and side lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, are lying on a smooth table as shown. What is the pressure exerted by the cubes on the table?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing two cubes labeled M1 and M2 with lengths L1 and L2 on a table surface

steps: Step 1: Identify that pressure is force per unit area: P = F/A
Step 2: The force each cube exerts on the table is its weight: F₁ = M₁g, F₂ = M₂g
Step 3: The contact area for each cube is its base area: A₁ = L₁², A₂ = L₂²
Step 4: Calculate pressure from each cube: P₁ = M₁g/L₁², P₂ = M₂g/L₂²
Step 5: Total pressure is the sum: P_total = M₁g/L₁² + M₂g/L₂²
solution.explanation: Pressure equals force divided by area. Each cube exerts a force equal to its weight (Mg) over an area equal to its base (L²), giving pressure Mg/L² for each cube.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Consider an object that floats in water but sinks in oil. When the object floats in water, half of it is submerged. If we slowly pour oil on top of the water so it completely covers the object, the object:

steps: Step 1: Initially, the object floats in water with half submerged, meaning $\rho_{object} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{water}$
Step 2: When oil is poured on top, it forms a layer above the water since oil is less dense than water
Step 3: The object will position itself at the oil-water interface to maintain equilibrium
Step 4: Since the object sinks in oil ($\rho_{object} > \rho_{oil}$) but floats in water ($\rho_{object} < \rho_{water}$), it will sink through the oil layer
Step 5: The object moves down until it reaches the oil-water interface where buoyant forces from both fluids can support its weight
Step 6: At equilibrium, part of the object is submerged in water (providing most buoyancy) and part in oil
solution.explanation: The object moves down because it sinks in the less dense oil layer until it reaches the oil-water interface where combined buoyant forces can support it

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: The pressure in a stream of water flowing out of a faucet is:

choices.c: the same everywhere in the stream

questions.diagram_description: Diagram of a water faucet with stream flowing downward

concepts: Conservation of energy in fluid flow
steps: Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation to the water stream. The stream is open to atmosphere, so pressure is atmospheric throughout.
Step 2: As water falls under gravity, it gains speed according to conservation of energy.
Step 3: By the continuity equation, as speed increases, cross-sectional area decreases (stream narrows).
Step 4: Since the stream is exposed to atmospheric pressure at all points, the pressure remains constant throughout the stream.
Step 5: The pressure is atmospheric everywhere in the stream, making it the same at all points.
solution.explanation: In a free-falling stream of water exposed to air, the pressure is atmospheric pressure everywhere. The stream narrows as it falls due to increasing velocity, but pressure remains constant.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Mass spectrometry is a way of measuring the mass-to-charge ratio of charged particles as well as distinguishing different components of a beam of particles. As an example of such an experiment, consider ions of two isotopes of Potassium, K₃₉⁺ and K₄₁⁺, with positive charge +e entering as a beam of particles into a region with magnetic field B in the z direction, as shown in the picture. The beam is injected at point O. Due to the magnetic field, all the ions travel in a circle with radius given by r = mv/qB, in which v is the velocity of ions, m is the mass of ions and q is the charge. After these two types of ions travel through the magnetic field, they reach a sensitive film and record bright spots on it as show in the picture. Consider the distance from point O to the bright spot produced by K₃₉⁺ ion to be L₁, and the distance to the spot recorded by K₄₁⁺ ion to be L₂. We use L for the average of L₁ and L₂.

parts.label: a
parts.text: Assuming that the energy of both isotopes is the same, compute the ratio (L₁-L₂)/L.

parts.label: b
parts.text: Since these ions have different masses, one can distinguish them by observing the two bright spots on the film. However, some experimental uncertainties can complicate the situation. For example, tuning the energy of the beam to a specific value is impossible. If the energy of particles in the beam varies from 7.9 × 10⁻¹⁷ J to 8.1 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, is it possible to distinguish these two types of ions?

parts.label: c
parts.text: Furthermore, adjusting the ions to enter exactly in the y direction, as shown in the picture, is also impossible. Assuming that the beam enters at a small angle α from the y direction, compute L as a function of α.

parts.label: d
parts.text: Now assume that the particles in the incident beam can have any angle α between 0 and 3° from the y direction, due to the uncertainty in the angle. Is it possible to distinguish the two types of ions in this case?

questions.given_data: r = mv/qB where v is velocity, m is mass, q is charge
questions.diagram_description: A coordinate system showing point O with a magnetic field B in the z direction, ions entering in the y direction, and a sensitive film positioned to detect the particles after they travel through circular paths

parts.label: a
parts.approach: Use equal kinetic energy to find velocity ratio, then radius ratio, then distance ratio
steps: Step 1: For equal energies, $\frac{1}{2}m_1v_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}m_2v_2^2$
Step 2: This gives $v_1/v_2 = \sqrt{m_2/m_1} = \sqrt{41/39}$
Step 3: Radius ratio: $r_1/r_2 = \frac{m_1v_1}{m_2v_2} = \frac{m_1}{m_2} \cdot \frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{39}{41} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{41}{39}} = \sqrt{\frac{39}{41}}$
Step 4: Distance ratio: $L_1 = 2r_1$, $L_2 = 2r_2$
Step 5: Calculate $\frac{L_1 - L_2}{L} = \frac{2(r_1 - r_2)}{r_1 + r_2} = \frac{r_1/r_2 - 1}{r_1/r_2 + 1}$
Step 6: Substitute: $\frac{\sqrt{39/41} - 1}{\sqrt{39/41} + 1} \approx -0.0256$

parts.label: b
parts.approach: Calculate the range of possible L values for each isotope due to energy uncertainty
steps: Step 1: Energy range gives velocity range: $v = \sqrt{2E/m}$
Step 2: For K₃₉⁺: $v_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 7.9 \times 10^{-17}/(39 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27})}$
Step 3: Calculate radius ranges for both isotopes
Step 4: Determine if L₁ range overlaps with L₂ range
Step 5: The energy uncertainty creates overlapping ranges
parts.answer: No, the energy uncertainty creates overlapping distance ranges that prevent clear distinction

parts.label: c
parts.approach: Use geometry to find how entrance angle affects the measured distance
steps: Step 1: With angle α from y-direction, the ion follows circular path with same radius r
Step 2: The center of circular path shifts horizontally by $r\sin(\alpha)$
Step 3: Use geometry to find intersection with detection film
Step 4: Distance from origin: $L(\alpha) = 2r\cos(\alpha)$
parts.answer: $L(\alpha) = 2r\cos(\alpha)$

parts.label: d
parts.approach: Determine if angular uncertainty creates overlapping ranges
steps: Step 1: For 0° to 3° range, $\cos(\alpha)$ varies from 1 to $\cos(3°) \approx 0.9986$
Step 2: This creates ~0.14% variation in L for each isotope
Step 3: Compare this variation to the separation found in part (a) (~2.6%)
Step 4: The angular uncertainty (0.14%) is much smaller than the isotope separation (2.6%)
parts.answer: Yes, the angular uncertainty creates only ~0.14% variation, which is much smaller than the ~2.6% separation between isotopes

solution.explanation: This problem analyzes a magnetic mass spectrometer where charged particles follow circular paths. The key insight is that particles with the same charge but different masses will have different radii when moving at the same speed, allowing isotope separation. Various experimental uncertainties are examined to determine if they prevent successful isotope identification.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text:
Problem 2 (10 points)
Andrzej's electric bike is equipped with a motor built into the front wheel hub. The stator of the electric motor (stationary part with the coils) is fixed solidly to the axle and the magnets are attached to the wheel. He made the following measurements on the bike:

1. If he runs it on a horizontal road at 20 km/h and then switches the motor off, it takes the bike 50 m to stop.
2. At full power, it can accelerate up a 5° slope from 5 to 20 km/h in 6 seconds.
3. The total mass of the bike plus the rider, is 106 kg.
4. The diameter of the wheels is 66 cm.
5. The diameter of the front wheel axle is 11 mm.
6. The bike uses a 48 V battery with a capacity of 8 Ah (ampere-hour).
7. The motor is 85% efficient.

Find out:
(a) What is the current flowing through the motor when the bike is driven at 20 km/h on a horizontal road without pedaling?
(b) How far can it go on one battery charge at 20 km/h?
(c) What is the net torque acting on the front axle?
(d) What is the current flowing through the motor when the bike is accelerating up a 5° slope from 0 to 20 km/h in 20 seconds?
(e) What is the full mechanical power of this motor?
(f) What is the maximum slope one can bike up at constant speed using the motor only (not pedaling)?
(g) What is the current flowing through the motor at maximum power?
(h) What was the most important simplifying assumption that you had to make in order to solve this problem?

parts.text: If he runs it on a horizontal road at 20 km/h and then switches the motor off, it takes the bike 50 m to stop.
At full power, it can accelerate up a 5° slope from 5 to 20 km/h in 6 seconds.
The total mass of the bike plus the rider, is 106 kg.
The diameter of the wheels is 66 cm.
The diameter of the front wheel axle is 11 mm.
The bike uses a 48 V battery with a capacity of 8 Ah (ampere-hour).
The motor is 85% efficient.

questions.given_data: The motor is 85% efficient

parts.label: a
parts.approach: Find rolling resistance from deceleration data, then calculate power needed at constant speed
steps: Step 1: Convert 20 km/h = 5.56 m/s
Step 2: Find deceleration: $a = \frac{v^2 - v_0^2}{2s} = \frac{0 - (5.56)^2}{2(50)} = -0.309$ m/s²
Step 3: Rolling resistance force: $F_r = ma = 106 \times 0.309 = 32.8$ N
Step 4: Power needed at constant speed: $P_{mech} = F_r v = 32.8 \times 5.56 = 182$ W
Step 5: Electrical power: $P_{elec} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta} = \frac{182}{0.85} = 214$ W
Step 6: Current: $I = \frac{P_{elec}}{V} = \frac{214}{48} = 4.46$ A

parts.label: b
parts.approach: Calculate range using battery capacity and current consumption
steps: Step 1: Battery energy = 48V × 8Ah = 384 Wh
Step 2: Time at 20 km/h: $t = \frac{384 \text{ Wh}}{214 \text{ W}} = 1.79$ h
Step 3: Distance: $d = vt = 20 \times 1.79 = 35.8$ km

parts.label: c
parts.approach: Calculate torque from force and wheel radius
steps: Step 1: Wheel radius: $r = \frac{0.66}{2} = 0.33$ m
Step 2: Net torque: $\tau = F_r \times r = 32.8 \times 0.33 = 10.8$ N⋅m

parts.label: d
parts.approach: Calculate total force needed for acceleration up slope
steps: Step 1: Acceleration: $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{5.56}{20} = 0.278$ m/s²
Step 2: Force for acceleration: $F_a = ma = 106 \times 0.278 = 29.5$ N
Step 3: Force for slope: $F_s = mg\sin(5°) = 106 \times 9.8 \times 0.087 = 90.4$ N
Step 4: Total force: $F_{total} = F_a + F_s + F_r = 29.5 + 90.4 + 32.8 = 152.7$ N
Step 5: Average speed = 2.78 m/s, Power = 152.7 × 2.78 = 424 W
Step 6: Electrical power = 424/0.85 = 499 W
Step 7: Current: $I = \frac{499}{48} = 10.4$ A

parts.label: e
parts.approach: Use maximum current capacity to find maximum power
steps: Step 1: Assume maximum safe current ≈ 1C = 8A (battery capacity)
Step 2: Maximum electrical power = 48V × 8A = 384 W
Step 3: Maximum mechanical power = 384 × 0.85 = 326 W
parts.answer: 326 W (assuming reasonable current limit)

parts.label: f
parts.approach: Find maximum slope angle using maximum power
steps: Step 1: At maximum slope, all power goes to overcome gravity and rolling resistance
Step 2: $P_{max} = (mg\sin\theta + F_r)v$
Step 3: $326 = (106 \times 9.8 \times \sin\theta + 32.8) \times 5.56$
Step 5: $\sin\theta = 0.0248$, $\theta = 1.42°$

parts.label: g
parts.approach: Current at maximum power already calculated
steps: Step 1: From part (e), maximum electrical power = 384 W
Step 2: Current = 384W / 48V = 8 A
parts.answer: 8 A

parts.label: h
parts.approach: Identify key simplifying assumptions
steps: Step 1: Assumed rolling resistance is constant at all speeds
Step 2: Neglected air resistance (major simplification)
Step 3: Assumed motor efficiency is constant
parts.answer: Neglecting air resistance, which becomes significant at higher speeds

solution.explanation: This problem involves analyzing an electric bike system by combining mechanical dynamics, energy considerations, and electrical power relationships. The key is to use the deceleration data to determine rolling resistance, then apply this to various operating scenarios.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: This problem has two independent parts.

parts.label: A
parts.text: A Russian nuclear icebreaker named 50 Let Pobedy has a length of 160 m, a width of 30 m and two nuclear reactors, each delivering about 30 MW of power. It can clear a path 35 m wide in 2 m thick ice (at −10°C) at a speed of 5 knots (2.5 m/s). Somebody suggested that instead of breaking the ice, one should melt it with a gigantic heater mounted at the front of the ship. (a) For this to work, what power should this heater have, in order to achieve the same performance as the icebreaker? (b) Explain in one sentence whether such a heater would work or not on this icebreaker.

parts.label: B
parts.text: You are given a graph of temperature as a function of time of a container with a mixture of ice and water slowly heated at constant rate. When all the ice melts, there is 850 mL of water in the container. In the following questions, neglect the evaporation of water. (a) At which rate is the container heated? (b) How much ice was initially present?

questions.given_data: When all the ice melts, there is 850 mL of water in the container
questions.diagram_description: A graph showing temperature (in degrees C) as a function of time, with temperature on y-axis ranging from approximately -1.75 to 7.00, and time in seconds on x-axis ranging from 0 to 1500. The graph shows a flat portion at 0°C (phase transition) followed by a linear increase in temperature.

parts.label: 3A(a)
parts.approach: Calculate energy needed to melt ice volume per unit time, then find required power
steps: Step 1: Calculate volume of ice to be melted per second: V = (width × thickness × speed) = 35 m × 2 m × 2.5 m/s = 175 m³/s
Step 2: Calculate mass of ice per second: m = ρV = 917 kg/m³ × 175 m³/s = 160,475 kg/s
Step 3: Calculate energy to warm ice from -10°C to 0°C: Q₁ = mc_ice ΔT = m × 2100 J/(kg·K) × 10 K
Step 4: Calculate energy to melt ice at 0°C: Q₂ = mL_f = m × 334,000 J/kg
Step 5: Total energy per unit time: P = (Q₁ + Q₂)/t = m(c_ice ΔT + L_f) = 160,475 × (21,000 + 334,000) = 57.0 GW
parts.answer: The heater would need approximately 57 GW of power

parts.label: 3A(b)
parts.approach: Compare required power with available power from reactors
steps: Step 1: Available power = 2 × 30 MW = 60 MW
Step 2: Required power = 57 GW = 57,000 MW
Step 3: Compare: Required power is ~950 times greater than available power
parts.answer: Such a heater would not work because the required power (57 GW) is nearly 1000 times greater than the available power from the reactors (60 MW)

parts.label: 3B(a)
parts.approach: Use the graph to determine heating rate from the linear portion after melting
steps: Step 1: From the graph, identify the linear region after ice melts (after ~750s)
Step 3: Use P = mc(ΔT/Δt) with m = 0.85 kg water: P = 0.85 kg × 4186 J/(kg·K) × 0.007 K/s ≈ 25 W
parts.answer: The container is heated at a rate of approximately 25 W

parts.label: 3B(b)
parts.approach: Use the duration of the melting plateau and heating rate to find initial ice mass
steps: Step 1: From graph, melting occurs from ~375s to ~750s, duration = 375s
Step 2: Energy used for melting = P × t = 25 W × 375 s = 9375 J
Step 3: Mass of ice melted = Q/L_f = 9375 J / 334,000 J/kg = 0.028 kg = 28 g
Step 4: Convert to volume: V = m/ρ = 0.028 kg / 917 kg/m³ ≈ 31 mL of ice
parts.answer: Approximately 28 g (or 31 mL) of ice was initially present

solution.explanation: This problem combines thermal physics concepts including phase changes, heat capacity, and power calculations. Part A involves calculating the enormous power needed to melt ice for an icebreaker, while Part B uses calorimetry to analyze a heating curve and determine initial conditions.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A small block of mass $m_1$ is released from rest at the top of a curve-shaped, frictionless wedge of mass $m_2$ which sits on a frictionless horizontal surface as shown. When the block leaves the wedge its velocity is measured to be 4.00 m/s to the right as shown in the figure. If the mass of the block is doubled to become 2$m_1$, what can be said about the speed with which it leaves the wedge?

choices.a: Its speed is less than 4.00 m/s
choices.b: Its speed is equal to 4.00 m/s
choices.c: Its speed is greater than 4.00 m/s

questions.diagram_description: Two-part diagram showing a block sliding down a curved frictionless wedge, with (a) showing initial position and (b) showing the block leaving the wedge with velocity 4.00 m/s to the right

steps: Step 1: Apply conservation of momentum. Initially both masses are at rest, so total momentum is zero. When the block leaves: $m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 = 0$, so $v_2 = -\frac{m_1}{m_2}v_1$

Step 2: Apply conservation of energy. Initial energy is gravitational potential energy $m_1 g h$. Final energy is kinetic energy of both masses: $m_1 g h = \frac{1}{2}m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 v_2^2$
Step 3: Substitute momentum relation into energy equation: $m_1 g h = \frac{1}{2}m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2(\frac{m_1}{m_2}v_1)^2 = \frac{1}{2}m_1 v_1^2(1 + \frac{m_1}{m_2})$
Step 4: Solve for $v_1$: $v_1^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 + \frac{m_1}{m_2}} = \frac{2gh m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$
Step 5: For doubled mass $2m_1$: $v_1'^2 = \frac{2gh m_2}{2m_1 + m_2}$
Step 6: Compare speeds. Since $2m_1 + m_2 > m_1 + m_2$, we have $v_1'^2 < v_1^2$, so $v_1' < v_1 = 4.00$ m/s
solution.explanation: When the mass is doubled, the speed decreases because more momentum is transferred to the wedge, leaving less kinetic energy for the block's motion.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Consider the electric charges A, B, C shown in the figure below, where $q$ is a positive number. Which answer correctly describes the magnitude of the net force experienced by the charges?
questions.diagram_description: Linear arrangement of three electric charges A, B, and C, where A has charge -q, B has charge +q, and C has charge +q, separated by equal distances d

steps: Step 1: Identify the charges - A has charge -q, B has charge +q, C has charge +q

Step 2: Calculate force on A - experiences attraction to B at distance d: $F_{AB} = k\frac{q^2}{d^2}$ (rightward), and repulsion from C at distance 2d: $F_{AC} = k\frac{q^2}{(2d)^2} = k\frac{q^2}{4d^2}$ (leftward). Net force: $F_A = k\frac{q^2}{d^2} - k\frac{q^2}{4d^2} = k\frac{3q^2}{4d^2}$

Step 3: Calculate force on B - experiences attraction to A at distance d: $F_{BA} = k\frac{q^2}{d^2}$ (leftward), and repulsion from C at distance d: $F_{BC} = k\frac{q^2}{d^2}$ (leftward). Net force: $F_B = k\frac{q^2}{d^2} + k\frac{q^2}{d^2} = k\frac{2q^2}{d^2}$

Step 4: Calculate force on C - experiences repulsion from B at distance d: $F_{CB} = k\frac{q^2}{d^2}$ (rightward), and repulsion from A at distance 2d: $F_{CA} = k\frac{q^2}{4d^2}$ (rightward). Net force: $F_C = k\frac{q^2}{d^2} + k\frac{q^2}{4d^2} = k\frac{5q^2}{4d^2}$

Step 5: Compare magnitudes - $F_B = k\frac{2q^2}{d^2}$, $F_C = k\frac{5q^2}{4d^2}$, $F_A = k\frac{3q^2}{4d^2}$. Since $\frac{2q^2}{d^2} > \frac{5q^2}{4d^2} > \frac{3q^2}{4d^2}$, we have $F_B > F_C > F_A$
solution.explanation: Force on B is largest due to equal contributions from both neighbors at distance d. Force on C is intermediate, and force on A is smallest due to partial cancellation between attraction to B and repulsion from distant C.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A long, straight wire carries a current that decreases linearly with time. What is the direction of the induced electric field outside the wire?

choices.a: Parallel to the current in the wire, in the same direction.
choices.b: Parallel to the current in the wire, in the opposite direction.
choices.c: Pointing radially outward from the wire.
choices.d: Pointing radially inward toward the wire.
choices.e: There is no induced electric field outside the wire.

steps: Step 1: Apply Faraday's law in integral form: $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$
Step 2: For a long straight wire, the magnetic field has cylindrical symmetry and points in circles around the wire according to the right-hand rule
Step 3: As current decreases with time, $\frac{dI}{dt} < 0$, so the magnetic flux through any loop around the wire is decreasing
Step 4: By Lenz's law, the induced electric field will try to oppose this change by creating a magnetic field in the same direction as the original field
Step 5: Due to cylindrical symmetry, the induced electric field must be tangential to circles centered on the wire
Step 6: Using the right-hand rule and the fact that the magnetic flux is decreasing, the induced electric field circulates in the same direction as the original current
solution.explanation: The induced electric field circulates around the wire in the same direction as the current due to Faraday's law and Lenz's law applied to the decreasing magnetic flux.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A car driven at a constant speed turns left. Which force makes the car turn?

choices.a: The force of friction, directed towards the left.
choices.b: The force of friction, directed towards the right.
choices.c: The force with which the driver is turning the steering wheel, directed towards the left.
choices.d: The force with which the driver is turning the steering wheel, directed towards the right.

steps: Step 1: When a car turns left at constant speed, it is undergoing circular motion
Step 2: Circular motion requires a centripetal force directed toward the center of the circular path
Step 3: For a left turn, the centripetal force must be directed toward the left (toward the center of the turn)
Step 4: The only horizontal force available to provide this centripetal force is the friction between the tires and the road
Step 5: The steering wheel changes the direction the wheels point, but the actual force that turns the car is the friction force between tires and road
Step 6: This friction force acts on the tires in the direction toward the center of the turn (leftward for a left turn)
solution.explanation: The friction force between the tires and road provides the centripetal force needed for circular motion, directed toward the center of the turn (left for a left turn).

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A cyclist stops pedaling at a velocity $v = 36$ km/h and notices that the bike has been moving for $d = 500$ m before it stops. The total mass of the bike, the biker and her camping equipment is $m = 100$ kg. What is the average combined force on the bicycle due to friction and drag?

steps: Step 1: Convert initial velocity to m/s: v₀ = 36 km/h = 36/3.6 = 10 m/s
Step 2: Use kinematic equation to find acceleration: v² = v₀² + 2ad, where v = 0 (final), v₀ = 10 m/s, d = 500 m
Step 3: Solve for acceleration: 0 = 100 + 2a(500), so a = -100/1000 = -0.1 m/s²
Step 4: Apply Newton's second law: F = ma = 100 kg × (-0.1 m/s²) = -10 N
Step 5: The magnitude of the average combined force is 10 N
solution.explanation: Using kinematics to find deceleration and Newton's second law, the average friction and drag force is 10 N opposing motion

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A child is swinging to and fro on a playground swing. At the instant the chains of the swing are vertical, what is the direction of the child's acceleration?

choices.d: Opposite to the direction of motion.
choices.e: At that moment, the acceleration is zero.

steps: Step 1: At the bottom of the swing when chains are vertical, the child is moving in circular motion.
Step 2: The forces acting on the child are tension T (upward) and weight mg (downward).
Step 3: For circular motion, there must be net centripetal acceleration toward the center of the circle (upward).
Step 4: Apply Newton's second law: T - mg = ma_c, where a_c is centripetal acceleration.
Step 5: Since the child has speed v at the bottom, a_c = v²/r > 0, so net acceleration is upward.
Step 6: The tension must be greater than weight to provide the centripetal force.
solution.explanation: At the bottom of the swing, the child requires centripetal acceleration directed toward the center of the circular path (upward). The tension in the chains must exceed the child's weight to provide this upward net force.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A bullet of mass 5 g is accelerated in a rifle barrel with an approximately constant force of 2500 N. The mass of the rifle is 5 kg. What is the force pushing the rifle back?

steps: Step 1: Identify that this is a Newton's Third Law problem where the bullet and rifle form an action-reaction pair
Step 2: Apply Newton's Third Law - for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force
Step 3: The force accelerating the bullet forward is 2500 N, so by Newton's Third Law, the force pushing the rifle backward must be equal in magnitude
Step 4: The force pushing the rifle back is 2500 N (same magnitude as the force on the bullet)
solution.explanation: By Newton's Third Law, the force pushing the rifle back equals the force pushing the bullet forward: 2500 N

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two balls of different masses collide head-on. After the collision, the balls remain at rest, and no external forces act on them. Which statement is true about the speeds of the balls before the collision?

choices.a: They must have been different, and the collision was inelastic.
choices.b: They must have been different, and the collision was elastic.
choices.c: They must have been identical, and the collision was inelastic.
choices.d: They must have been identical, and the collision was elastic.
choices.e: No combination of speed values could have caused both balls to stop after the collision.

steps: Step 1: Apply conservation of momentum. Initial momentum = Final momentum
Step 2: Since both balls are at rest after collision, final momentum = 0
Step 3: Therefore initial momentum must equal zero: $m_1v_1 + m_2v_2 = 0$
Step 4: This gives us $m_1v_1 = -m_2v_2$, so $v_1 = -\frac{m_2}{m_1}v_2$
Step 5: Since masses are different ($m_1 ≠ m_2$), the speeds must be different to satisfy momentum conservation
Step 6: Since both balls come to rest, kinetic energy is not conserved, making this an inelastic collision
solution.explanation: For momentum to be conserved with different masses ending at rest, initial speeds must be different and opposite in direction. Since kinetic energy is lost (balls come to rest), the collision is inelastic.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A Ferris wheel is turning about a horizontal axis through its center. The linear speed of a passenger sitting on the rim is constant. Which one of the following sentences about the passenger's acceleration is correct?

choices.a: Its magnitude at the highest point is higher than at the lowest point, and it is downwards at both points.
choices.b: Both its magnitude and direction are the same at the highest and at the lowest points.
choices.c: Its magnitude is the same at the highest and at the lowest points, but the directions are opposite.
choices.d: Its magnitude at the highest point is smaller than at the lowest point, and it is downwards at both points.

steps: Step 1: Identify that the passenger undergoes uniform circular motion since linear speed is constant.
Step 2: Apply centripetal acceleration formula $a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}$ where v is linear speed and r is radius.
Step 3: Since both v and r are constant throughout the motion, the magnitude of acceleration is constant: $|a_c| = \frac{v^2}{r}$ at all points.
Step 4: Analyze direction - centripetal acceleration always points toward the center of the circle.
Step 5: At the highest point, acceleration points downward (toward center). At the lowest point, acceleration points upward (toward center).
Step 6: Therefore, magnitude is the same at both points, but directions are opposite (down vs up).
solution.explanation: In uniform circular motion, centripetal acceleration has constant magnitude v²/r but direction always points toward the center, making directions opposite at highest and lowest points.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A uniform rod is suspended on two thin strings as shown below. Which string has a larger tension force?

choices.c: The tension force is the same on both

questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing a uniform rod suspended by two thin strings - one vertical string from the center and one angled string from the left side

steps: Step 1: Identify the system - uniform rod suspended by two strings, one vertical (right) and one at an angle (left)
Step 2: Apply equilibrium conditions. For vertical force balance: $T_1 \sin\theta + T_2 = mg$ where $T_1$ is tension in angled string, $T_2$ is tension in vertical string
Step 3: Apply torque balance about the center of mass. The angled string creates a longer moment arm than the vertical string
Step 4: Since the rod is uniform, its center of mass is at the geometric center. The angled string is farther from the center than the vertical string
Step 5: For torque equilibrium, the string with the shorter moment arm (vertical string on right) must have larger tension to balance the torque from the angled string
Step 6: The vertical string carries more load because it's closer to the center of mass and has a more direct upward pull
solution.explanation: The right (vertical) string has larger tension because it has a shorter moment arm from the center of mass and must provide more upward force to maintain equilibrium

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two point charges $-2Q$ and $+Q$ are placed on the $x$-axis with $-2Q$ at $x = 0$ and $+Q$ at $x = a$. Which of the following statements is true?

choices.a: There is no point on the $x$-axis where the electric field is zero.
choices.b: There is a point on the $x$-axis, at $x < 0$, where the electric field is zero.
choices.c: There is a point on the $x$-axis, between the charges $(0 < x < a)$, where the electric field is zero.
choices.d: There is a point on the $x$-axis, at $x > a$, where the electric field is zero.

steps: Step 1: Set up the problem with charge -2Q at x=0 and +Q at x=a
Step 2: Analyze each region. For x < 0: both fields point left (same direction), so they cannot cancel
Step 3: For 0 < x < a: field from -2Q points right, field from +Q points left, so they can potentially cancel
Step 4: For x > a: both fields point right (same direction), so they cannot cancel
Step 5: Set up equation for cancellation between charges: $k\frac{2Q}{x^2} = k\frac{Q}{(a-x)^2}$
Step 6: Solve: $\frac{2}{x^2} = \frac{1}{(a-x)^2}$, which gives $\sqrt{2}(a-x) = x$
Step 7: This yields $x = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{1+\sqrt{2}}$, which is between 0 and a
solution.explanation: The electric field can only be zero between the charges where the fields point in opposite directions and can cancel each other out.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: In a lab experiment, a laser beam hits a semicircular glass object off the centre axis. The ray enters perpendicularly to the surface, and the environment is air. Which ray diagram is correct?
questions.diagram_description: Six ray diagrams (a-f) showing laser beam paths through semicircular glass objects

steps: Step 1: Ray enters perpendicular to curved surface, so no refraction occurs at entry
Step 2: Ray travels through glass and hits the flat surface at some angle
Step 3: Since glass has higher refractive index than air (n_glass > n_air), total internal reflection can occur
Step 4: If angle of incidence at flat surface exceeds critical angle, total internal reflection occurs
Step 5: Ray hits off-center, so angle at flat surface will be significant
Step 6: Option B shows total internal reflection at the flat surface with ray exiting through curved surface
solution.explanation: The ray enters perpendicular to the curved surface (no bending), travels through the glass, and undergoes total internal reflection at the flat surface because the angle exceeds the critical angle for glass-air interface.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: You put two identical ice cubes on plates of different materials. One cube is put on an aluminium plate, and the other on a glass plate. Both plates have been in the room for a long time prior to the experiment. You notice that the ice melts faster on the metal plate. Why?

choices.a: The ice is in thermal equilibrium with the plastic plate, but not with the metal plate.
choices.b: The metal plate conducts heat to the ice more rapidly than the plastic plate.
choices.c: The metal plate holds more heat than the plastic plate.
choices.d: The metal plate was warmer than the plastic plate initially.

steps: Step 1: Both plates have been in the room for a long time, so they are at the same initial temperature (room temperature).
Step 2: When ice is placed on both plates, heat flows from the warmer plates to the colder ice.
Step 3: The rate of heat transfer depends on the thermal conductivity of the material: q = kA(ΔT/Δx), where k is thermal conductivity.
Step 4: Metals (like aluminum) have much higher thermal conductivity than glass or plastic.
Step 5: The aluminum plate can conduct heat to the ice much faster than the glass plate, causing faster melting.
Step 6: Options (a), (c), and (d) are incorrect because both plates start at room temperature and the difference is in conduction rate, not heat capacity or initial temperature.
solution.explanation: The metal plate conducts heat to the ice more rapidly due to its higher thermal conductivity compared to glass/plastic, resulting in faster melting of the ice cube on the metal plate.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two black objects of the same diameter, a sphere and a flat disk, are placed in a parallel beam of light. The plane of the disk is perpendicular to the beam. What can be said about the force acting on them?

choices.b: The force is larger on the disk than on the sphere.
choices.c: The force is larger on the sphere than on the disk.
choices.d: The force is the same on both objects.

steps: Step 1: When light hits an object, it exerts radiation pressure due to photon momentum transfer.
Step 2: The force depends on the power of light intercepted: F = P/c, where P is intercepted power and c is speed of light.
Step 3: Power intercepted equals intensity times cross-sectional area: P = I × A.
Step 4: Both objects have the same diameter, so both present the same cross-sectional area to the parallel beam: A_sphere = A_disk = πr².
Step 5: Since both are black objects (perfect absorbers) with identical cross-sectional areas in the same beam, they intercept the same power.
Step 6: Therefore, both experience the same radiation pressure force.
solution.explanation: Both objects have identical cross-sectional areas perpendicular to the beam and are both black (perfect absorbers), so they intercept the same power and experience the same radiation pressure force.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Adrianne has a device that emits photons with a wavelength of λ = 1.498 km. Kwan has a similar device that emits photons at a frequency of f = 201 kHz. Whose device emits photons in air at the highest frequency?

choices.d: There is not enough information to compare.

steps: Step 1: Use the wave equation c = fλ where c is the speed of light in air (≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s)
Step 2: Convert Adrianne's wavelength to meters: λ = 1.498 km = 1498 m
Step 3: Calculate Adrianne's frequency: f_A = c/λ = (3.0 × 10⁸ m/s)/(1498 m) = 2.00 × 10⁵ Hz = 200 kHz
Step 4: Kwan's frequency is given as f_K = 201 kHz
Step 5: Compare frequencies: 201 kHz > 200 kHz, so Kwan's device has the higher frequency
solution.explanation: Kwan's device emits photons at 201 kHz while Adrianne's device emits at 200 kHz (calculated from wavelength), so Kwan's has the higher frequency

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A girl sitting on a floating mattress in a small pool holds a model of a motorboat in her hands. When she puts it in the water to start driving it with her remote control, what happens to the water level in the pool?

choices.a: It rises by a small amount.
choices.b: It drops by a small amount.
choices.d: The qualitative result depends on the weight of the model boat.

steps: Step 1: Initially, the girl and model boat are on the floating mattress. The mattress displaces water equal to the total weight (girl + mattress + boat) divided by water density.
Step 2: When the boat is placed in water, it floats and displaces water equal to its own weight divided by water density.
Step 3: The mattress now only needs to support the girl's weight, so it displaces less water than before.
Step 4: The key insight is that when the boat was on the mattress, it caused displacement equal to its full weight. Now floating separately, it only displaces water equal to its weight.
Step 5: Since the boat's weight hasn't changed, the total displacement remains the same - the boat still displaces the same volume of water whether it's on the mattress or floating separately.
Step 6: Therefore, the water level in the pool stays the same.
solution.explanation: The total water displacement remains constant because the boat's weight hasn't changed - it still displaces the same volume of water whether carried by the mattress or floating independently.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Which of the accompanying P–V diagrams best represents an isothermal process, that is a process occurring at constant temperature?
questions.diagram_description: Five P-V diagrams (a-e) showing different thermodynamic processes

steps: Step 1: For an isothermal process, temperature T remains constant
Step 2: From ideal gas law PV = nRT, if T is constant and n is fixed, then PV = constant
Step 3: This means P is inversely proportional to V: P ∝ 1/V
Step 4: On a P-V diagram, this relationship appears as a hyperbolic curve
Step 5: Examining the options, diagram b) shows a hyperbolic curve characteristic of an isothermal process
solution.explanation: An isothermal process maintains constant temperature, so PV = constant, which graphs as a hyperbola on a P-V diagram

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two identical objects of mass m are connected to a massless string which is hung over two frictionless pulleys as shown below. If everything is at rest, what is the tension in the cord?

choices.c: More than mg, but less than 2mg.

questions.diagram_description: Pulley system diagram showing two masses m connected by string over two pulleys

steps: Step 1: Since the system is at rest, we have static equilibrium
Step 2: For each mass m, the forces acting are weight mg downward and tension T upward
Step 3: For equilibrium of each mass: T - mg = 0, therefore T = mg
Step 4: The tension is the same throughout the massless string
Step 5: Each segment of the string has tension T = mg
solution.explanation: In equilibrium, the tension in the string must balance the weight of each mass, so T = mg exactly.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Car A and car B are both traveling down a straight highway at 90 km/h. Car A is only 6.0 metres behind car B. The driver of car B brakes, slowing down with a constant deceleration of 2.0 m/s². After 1.2 seconds, the driver of car A begins to brake, also at 2.0 m/s², but this is insufficient and the cars eventually collide, both still moving forward. What is the relative velocity of the two cars at the moment of the collision?

steps: Step 1: Convert initial velocity: 90 km/h = 25 m/s
Step 2: Car B brakes for entire collision time t, Car A brakes for (t-1.2) seconds
Step 3: At collision, positions are equal: $25t - t^2 = 6 + 25t - \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot (t-1.2)^2$
Step 4: Simplify: $-t^2 = 6 - (t-1.2)^2$, which gives $t^2 - (t-1.2)^2 = 6$
Step 6: Solve for collision time: $t = \frac{7.44}{2.4} = 3.1$ seconds
Step 7: Find velocities at collision: $v_A = 25 - 2(3.1-1.2) = 21.2$ m/s, $v_B = 25 - 2(3.1) = 18.8$ m/s
Step 8: Relative velocity = $v_A - v_B = 21.2 - 18.8 = 2.4$ m/s
solution.explanation: Car A has a 1.2 second delay before braking, causing it to be moving 2.4 m/s faster than car B at collision

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two interfering waves have the same wavelength, frequency, and amplitude. They are traveling in the same direction but are 90 degrees out of phase. Compared to the individual waves, what can be said about the resultant wave?

choices.a: It will have the same amplitude and velocity, but a different wavelength.
choices.b: It will have the same amplitude and wavelength, but a different velocity.
choices.c: It will have the same wavelength and velocity, but a different amplitude.
choices.d: It will have the same amplitude and frequency, but a different velocity.
choices.e: It will have the same frequency and velocity, but a different wavelength.

steps: Step 1: Two waves with same amplitude A, wavelength λ, and frequency f are 90° out of phase
Step 2: For waves 90° out of phase, φ = 90°, so cos(90°) = 0
Step 3: Using superposition: A_resultant = √(A² + A² + 2A·A·cos(90°)) = √(2A²) = A√2
Step 4: The resultant amplitude is A√2, which is different from the original amplitude A
Step 5: Wave velocity v = fλ remains unchanged since the medium is the same
Step 6: Wavelength λ remains unchanged since frequency and velocity are unchanged
solution.explanation: When two waves of equal amplitude interfere 90° out of phase, the resultant has the same wavelength and velocity but different amplitude (√2 times larger)

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A spinning ice skater has an initial kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$. She pulls in her outstretched arms, decreasing her moment of inertia to $\frac{1}{4}I$. What is her new angular speed?

steps: Step 1: Apply conservation of angular momentum. Initial angular momentum: $L_i = I\omega$
Step 2: Final angular momentum: $L_f = \frac{1}{4}I\omega_f$
Step 3: Since angular momentum is conserved: $L_i = L_f$
Step 4: Set up equation: $I\omega = \frac{1}{4}I\omega_f$
Step 5: Cancel I from both sides: $\omega = \frac{1}{4}\omega_f$
Step 6: Solve for final angular speed: $\omega_f = 4\omega$
solution.explanation: When the ice skater pulls in her arms, her moment of inertia decreases by a factor of 4. By conservation of angular momentum, her angular speed must increase by a factor of 4 to maintain constant angular momentum.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A ball of mass $m$ is thrown vertically upward. Instead of neglecting air resistance, assume that the force of air resistance has a magnitude proportional to the ball's velocity, but pointing in the opposite direction. What is the ball's acceleration at the highest point?

steps: Step 1: At the highest point, the ball's velocity is zero (v = 0)
Step 2: Since air resistance is proportional to velocity (F_air = kv), when v = 0, the air resistance force is also zero
Step 3: With no air resistance at the highest point, the only force acting on the ball is gravity (mg downward)
Step 4: Apply Newton's second law: F_net = ma = mg
Step 5: Therefore, acceleration a = g (downward)
solution.explanation: At the highest point, velocity is zero so air resistance is zero, leaving only gravity acting on the ball, giving acceleration g.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A hypothetical planet has density $\rho$, radius $R$, and surface gravitational acceleration $g$. What would be the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of a planet with double the radius, and the same planetary density?

steps: Step 1: Start with gravitational acceleration formula $g = \frac{GM}{R^2}$
Step 2: Express mass in terms of density: $M = \rho V = \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$
Step 3: Substitute mass into gravity equation: $g = \frac{G \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{R^2} = \frac{4}{3}\pi G \rho R$
Step 4: For the original planet: $g_1 = \frac{4}{3}\pi G \rho R$
Step 5: For the new planet with radius $2R$ and same density $\rho$: $g_2 = \frac{4}{3}\pi G \rho (2R) = 2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi G \rho R = 2g_1$
Step 6: Therefore $g_2 = 2g$
solution.explanation: When density is constant and radius doubles, gravitational acceleration is proportional to radius, so it doubles to 2g

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: You are given two lenses placed close together: a converging lens with focal length +10 cm, and a diverging lens with focal length −20 cm. Which of the following would produce a virtual image that is larger than the object?

choices.a: Placing the object 5 cm from the converging lens.
choices.b: Placing the object 15 cm from the converging lens.
choices.c: Placing the object 25 cm from the converging lens.
choices.d: Placing the object 15 cm from the diverging lens.
choices.e: Placing the object 25 cm from the diverging lens.

steps: Step 1: Calculate equivalent focal length: $\frac{1}{f_{eq}} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{-20} = \frac{2-1}{20} = \frac{1}{20}$, so $f_{eq} = 20$ cm
Step 2: For virtual image larger than object, need $|m| > 1$ and negative $d_i$
Step 3: Check each option using $\frac{1}{20} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}$:
Step 7: Options d) and e) use individual diverging lens which always produces smaller virtual images
solution.explanation: When object is placed 5 cm from the converging lens in the lens combination, it produces a virtual image with magnification 1.33, making it larger than the object. All three positions with the lens combination work, but option a is the first correct answer.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: An electromagnetic wave is propagating in vacuum in the positive s direction. At an instant when its magnetic field B at a certain position is in the positive x direction and getting stronger, what happens to its electric field E at that same position?

choices.a: E is in the positive x direction and getting weaker.
choices.b: E is in the negative x direction and getting stronger.
choices.c: E is in the positive y direction and getting weaker.
choices.d: E is in the negative y direction and getting stronger.

steps: Step 1: For an EM wave propagating in +z direction, E and B are perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction.
Step 2: Given B is in +x direction and increasing (∂B/∂t > 0).
Step 3: From Faraday's law: ∇×E = -∂B/∂t. Since ∂B/∂t > 0 in +x direction, ∇×E points in -x direction.
Step 4: For a wave propagating in +z with B in +x, the curl of E in -x direction means E must be in -y direction.
Step 5: Since ∂B/∂t > 0, we have ∂E/∂t < 0, so E is getting stronger in the -y direction.
solution.explanation: Using Faraday's law and the properties of electromagnetic waves, when B is in +x and increasing, E must be in -y direction and getting stronger.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A light emitting diode (LED) is connected in series with a super capacitor of capacitance 3000 F and internal resistance ∼ 1 mΩ, and a microprocessor-controlled resistor. The capacitor is charged to 5 V. The LED requires constant current of 800 mA. At this current, the voltage drop over the LED is 2.7 V.

parts.label: a
parts.text: What should be the dependence of the variable resistor on time for the current to remain constant?

parts.label: b
parts.text: What should be the initial value of the resistor? Does the value of the resistor stabilize after some time, and if so, to which resistance?

parts.label: c
parts.text: How long can the LED operate at full brightness before the capacitor needs to be recharged?

questions.given_data: Capacitance = 3000 F, internal resistance ∼ 1 mΩ, initial capacitor voltage = 5 V, LED current = 800 mA, LED voltage drop = 2.7 V

parts.label: a
parts.approach: Use constant current discharge of capacitor to find how voltage changes with time, then apply Ohm's law
steps: For constant current I = 800 mA, the capacitor voltage decreases linearly: $V_C(t) = V_0 - \frac{I \cdot t}{C}$
Apply Kirchhoff's voltage law: $V_C(t) = V_{LED} + I \cdot R(t) + I \cdot r_{internal}$
Since internal resistance is negligible (~1 mΩ), we have: $V_C(t) = V_{LED} + I \cdot R(t)$
Solving for R(t): $R(t) = \frac{V_C(t) - V_{LED}}{I} = \frac{V_0 - \frac{I \cdot t}{C} - V_{LED}}{I}$
Substituting values: $R(t) = \frac{5 - \frac{0.8t}{3000} - 2.7}{0.8} = \frac{2.3 - \frac{0.8t}{3000}}{0.8}$
parts.answer: $R(t) = 2.875 - \frac{t}{3000}$ Ω (resistor must decrease linearly with time)

parts.label: b
parts.approach: Find initial resistance at t=0 and final resistance when capacitor voltage equals LED voltage
steps: Initial resistance at t=0: $R(0) = \frac{V_0 - V_{LED}}{I} = \frac{5 - 2.7}{0.8} = \frac{2.3}{0.8} = 2.875$ Ω
The LED can operate until $V_C(t) = V_{LED} = 2.7$ V
At this point, all capacitor voltage drops across the LED, so $R_{final} = 0$ Ω
This occurs when: $5 - \frac{0.8t}{3000} = 2.7$, solving gives $t = 8625$ s
parts.answer: Initial resistance: 2.875 Ω; Final resistance: 0 Ω (resistor approaches zero)

parts.label: c
parts.approach: Find time when capacitor voltage equals LED voltage (minimum operating voltage)
steps: LED stops operating when $V_C(t) = V_{LED} = 2.7$ V
Setting up equation: $5 - \frac{0.8t}{3000} = 2.7$
Therefore: $t = \frac{2.3 \times 3000}{0.8} = 8625$ s
Converting to hours: $t = \frac{8625}{3600} = 2.396$ hours ≈ 2.4 hours
parts.answer: 8625 seconds or approximately 2.4 hours

solution.explanation: This problem involves a capacitor discharging at constant current through an LED and variable resistor. The key insight is that for constant current discharge, the capacitor voltage decreases linearly with time, requiring the series resistance to decrease accordingly to maintain constant current through the LED.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A light spring with a force constant of 3.85 N/m is compressed by 8.00 cm and held between two blocks. The block on the left has a mass of 250 g, while the block on the right has a mass of 500 g. Both blocks are initially held at rest on a horizontal surface, and are then released simultaneously so that the spring pushes them apart.

parts.text: What is the maximum velocity each block attains if the coefficient of kinetic friction between each block and the surface is 0.100 and the coefficient of static friction is 0.120?

questions.given_data: Spring constant k = 3.85 N/m, compression = 8.00 cm, left block mass = 250 g, right block mass = 500 g, μₖ = 0.100, μₛ = 0.120

parts.label: main
parts.approach: Use conservation of momentum to find velocity relationship, then apply work-energy theorem accounting for friction losses
steps: Step 1: Calculate initial spring potential energy: $E_0 = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = \frac{1}{2}(3.85)(0.08)^2 = 0.01232$ J
Step 2: Apply conservation of momentum: $0 = m_1v_1 + m_2v_2$, so $v_2 = -\frac{m_1}{m_2}v_1 = -\frac{0.25}{0.5}v_1 = -0.5v_1$
Step 3: When spring returns to natural length, kinetic energies are: $KE_1 = \frac{1}{2}(0.25)v_1^2$ and $KE_2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.5)(0.5v_1)^2 = \frac{1}{16}(0.5)v_1^2$
Step 4: Total KE at separation: $KE_{total} = \frac{1}{8}v_1^2 + \frac{1}{16}v_1^2 = \frac{3}{16}v_1^2$
Step 5: By energy conservation during expansion: $0.01232 = \frac{3}{16}v_1^2$, solving: $v_1 = 0.813$ m/s, $v_2 = 0.407$ m/s
Step 6: After separation, friction decelerates each block. For block 1: $a_1 = -\mu_k g = -0.98$ m/s², stopping distance $d_1 = \frac{v_1^2}{2\mu_k g} = 0.337$ m
Step 7: For block 2: $a_2 = -\mu_k g = -0.98$ m/s², stopping distance $d_2 = \frac{v_2^2}{2\mu_k g} = 0.0843$ m
Step 8: Maximum velocities are the velocities at separation since friction only slows them down afterward

solution.explanation: This problem combines elastic potential energy, conservation of momentum, and friction. The spring's energy is converted to kinetic energy of both blocks, with momentum conservation determining the velocity ratio. The maximum velocities occur at the moment of separation, after which friction decelerates both blocks to rest.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: The luminosity of a star is defined as the energy it emits per unit time. We know from astronomical observations that the luminosity L of the stars that have a mass M < 10M☉ is approximately related to their mass as: L/L☉ = (M/M☉)³·⁵ where L☉ and M☉ are the luminosity and mass of the Sun. Furthermore, we know from Einstein's theory of relativity that the energy released from converting a mass M into energy is given by E = Mc². In this problem, we assume that all stars burn almost the same fraction of their mass during their active life.

parts.label: a
parts.text: If the fraction of the mass converted into energy is α ≪ 1, first find an expression for the lifetime of a star in terms of its mass, luminosity, α and physical constants.

parts.label: b
parts.text: Using the luminosity-mass equation, find the lifetime of a star as a function of its mass.

parts.label: c
parts.text: If we know that the lifetime of our Sun is about 10 billion years, what is the lifetime of a star with a mass M = 5M☉?

questions.given_data: L/L☉ = (M/M☉)³·⁵ (for M < 10M☉), E = Mc², α ≪ 1 (fraction of mass converted), Sun lifetime ≈ 10 billion years

parts.label: a
parts.approach: Use energy conservation: total energy available divided by energy consumption rate gives lifetime
steps: Step 1: Total energy available from mass conversion is $E_{total} = \alpha Mc^2$, where $\alpha$ is the fraction of mass converted
Step 2: Energy consumption rate is the luminosity $L$
Step 3: Lifetime is total energy divided by consumption rate: $t = \frac{E_{total}}{L} = \frac{\alpha Mc^2}{L}$
parts.answer: $t = \frac{\alpha Mc^2}{L}$

parts.label: b
parts.approach: Substitute the luminosity-mass relationship into the lifetime expression from part (a)
steps: Step 1: From part (a): $t = \frac{\alpha Mc^2}{L}$
Step 2: From the luminosity-mass relation: $L = L_{\odot}\left(\frac{M}{M_{\odot}}\right)^{3.5}$
Step 3: Substitute: $t = \frac{\alpha Mc^2}{L_{\odot}\left(\frac{M}{M_{\odot}}\right)^{3.5}}$
Step 4: Simplify: $t = \frac{\alpha Mc^2 M_{\odot}^{3.5}}{L_{\odot} M^{3.5}} = \frac{\alpha c^2 M_{\odot}^{3.5}}{L_{\odot}} M^{-2.5}$
Step 5: For the Sun ($M = M_{\odot}$): $t_{\odot} = \frac{\alpha M_{\odot}c^2}{L_{\odot}}$
Step 6: Therefore: $t = t_{\odot}\left(\frac{M}{M_{\odot}}\right)^{-2.5}$
parts.answer: $t = t_{\odot}\left(\frac{M}{M_{\odot}}\right)^{-2.5}$

parts.label: c
parts.approach: Apply the mass-lifetime relationship from part (b) with the given values
steps: Step 1: From part (b): $t = t_{\odot}\left(\frac{M}{M_{\odot}}\right)^{-2.5}$
Step 2: Given: $t_{\odot} = 10$ billion years, $M = 5M_{\odot}$
Step 3: Substitute: $t = 10 \text{ billion years} \times \left(\frac{5M_{\odot}}{M_{\odot}}\right)^{-2.5}$
Step 4: Calculate: $t = 10 \times 5^{-2.5} = 10 \times \frac{1}{5^{2.5}} = 10 \times \frac{1}{5^2 \sqrt{5}} = 10 \times \frac{1}{25\sqrt{5}}$
Step 5: Simplify: $t = \frac{10}{25\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{5\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2\sqrt{5}}{25} \approx 0.18$ billion years
parts.answer: Approximately 0.18 billion years (180 million years)

solution.explanation: This problem explores the relationship between stellar mass, luminosity, and lifetime. More massive stars are much more luminous but have shorter lifetimes because they burn through their fuel much faster. The key insight is that lifetime scales as M^(-2.5), meaning a 5 solar mass star lives about 56 times shorter than the Sun.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Consider the electric power dissipation due to resistance in a circuit. Which of the following changes leave the dissipated electric power unchanged?

choices.a: Doubling the voltage and reducing the current by a factor of two.
choices.b: Doubling the voltage and increasing the resistance by a factor of four.
choices.c: Doubling the current and reducing the resistance by a factor of four.

questions.diagram_description: Grid-based diagram showing what appears to be a broken glass piece with crack patterns on graph paper

steps: Step 1: Identify the three equivalent expressions for power: P = I²R, P = V²/R, P = VI
Step 2: Check option (a) - Double voltage, halve current: P = VI → P = (2V)(I/2) = VI. Power unchanged ✓
Step 3: Check option (b) - Double voltage, quadruple resistance: P = V²/R → P = (2V)²/(4R) = 4V²/(4R) = V²/R. Power unchanged ✓
Step 4: Check option (c) - Double current, quarter resistance: P = I²R → P = (2I)²(R/4) = 4I²(R/4) = I²R. Power unchanged ✓
Step 5: Since all three options (a), (b), and (c) keep power unchanged, the answer is (f)
solution.explanation: All three scenarios maintain constant power dissipation by compensating changes in one variable with appropriate changes in another variable according to the power equations.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A U-shaped glass tube closed at both ends and positioned vertically is partly filled with water. At a certain time the levels of water are different in the two arms of the tube due to a difference in air pressure above each arm. If there is no temperature change, what will happen to the water level on both sides?

choices.a: The water levels will stay stationary.
choices.b: The water levels will equalize rapidly — within seconds.
choices.c: The water levels will slowly equalize — within days.
choices.d: The difference between the levels in each arm will increase.

questions.diagram_description: U-shaped glass tube diagram showing a vertical tube closed at both ends and positioned vertically, partially filled with water

steps: Step 1: Analyze the initial condition - the tube is closed at both ends with trapped air above water in each arm at different pressures
Step 2: Since temperature is constant, the gas pressures will remain constant (no volume change possible in rigid closed system)
Step 3: For equilibrium, pressure at the bottom must be equal: P₁ + ρgh₁ = P₂ + ρgh₂
Step 4: Since P₁ ≠ P₂ initially and these pressures cannot change (closed system, constant T), the height difference h₂ - h₁ must remain constant
Step 5: The water levels will remain stationary because the pressure difference in the trapped air maintains the height difference
solution.explanation: In a closed U-tube with trapped air, the different air pressures above each water column maintain the height difference. Since temperature is constant and no air can enter or leave, the pressures remain fixed and the water levels stay stationary.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: How does the magnitude of the gravitational force with which the Moon attracts the Earth compare to the magnitude of the gravitational force with which the Earth attracts the Moon?

steps: Step 1: Apply Newton's law of universal gravitation. The gravitational force between Earth and Moon is $F = G\frac{M_E M_M}{r^2}$ where $M_E$ is Earth's mass, $M_M$ is Moon's mass, and $r$ is the distance between their centers.
Step 2: The force that Earth exerts on Moon has magnitude $F_{E \rightarrow M} = G\frac{M_E M_M}{r^2}$
Step 3: The force that Moon exerts on Earth has magnitude $F_{M \rightarrow E} = G\frac{M_M M_E}{r^2}$
Step 4: Since multiplication is commutative, $M_E M_M = M_M M_E$, so both forces have identical magnitudes.
Step 5: This is also consistent with Newton's Third Law: forces between two objects form an action-reaction pair with equal magnitudes but opposite directions.
solution.explanation: By Newton's Third Law and the law of universal gravitation, gravitational forces between two objects are always equal in magnitude regardless of their masses.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: The friction force acting on a bicycle is 20 N. What power does a cyclist need in order to travel at 18 km/h?

steps: Step 1: At constant velocity, the cyclist must apply a force equal to the friction force to maintain equilibrium: F_applied = F_friction = 20 N
Step 2: Convert velocity from km/h to m/s: v = 18 km/h × (1000 m/km) × (1 h/3600 s) = 5 m/s
Step 3: Apply the power formula: P = F × v = 20 N × 5 m/s = 100 W
solution.explanation: Power required equals force times velocity. At constant speed, applied force equals friction force.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: What is the average horizontal force acting on a ball when it elastically bounces off a wall, assuming the collision time is 0.1 s and the momentum of the ball before the bounce was 2 kg · m/s, perpendicular to the wall?

steps: Step 1: For an elastic collision with a wall, the ball reverses direction, so initial momentum = +2 kg·m/s and final momentum = -2 kg·m/s
Step 2: Calculate change in momentum: Δp = p_final - p_initial = (-2) - (+2) = -4 kg·m/s
Step 3: Apply impulse-momentum theorem: Δp = F_avg × Δt
Step 4: Solve for average force: F_avg = Δp/Δt = (-4 kg·m/s)/(0.1 s) = -40 N
Step 5: The magnitude of the average horizontal force is 40 N
solution.explanation: The elastic collision reverses the ball's momentum from +2 to -2 kg·m/s, giving a change of -4 kg·m/s. Using the impulse-momentum theorem with collision time 0.1 s gives an average force magnitude of 40 N.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: A small explosion occurs in a model airplane which rips it into two pieces. The model was flying with velocity $v$ just before the explosion. Which combination of $v_1$ and $v_2$ are possible velocities of the two pieces after the explosion?

choices.a: v₁ diagonal down-left, v₂ diagonal up-left
choices.b: v₁ diagonal up-left, v₂ diagonal down-left
choices.c: v₁ vertically up, v₂ vertically down

questions.diagram_description: Vector diagram showing explosion velocities with initial velocity v and resulting velocities v1 and v2 in three different scenarios (a, b, c)

steps: Step 1: Apply conservation of momentum. Initial momentum is mv in horizontal direction.
Step 2: Final momentum must equal initial momentum: $m_1\vec{v_1} + m_2\vec{v_2} = m\vec{v}$
Step 3: Analyze each option. The vector sum of v₁ and v₂ must equal the original velocity v (horizontal right).
Step 4: Option (a) shows v₁ down-left and v₂ up-left. Their vector sum can equal v horizontal.
Step 5: Option (b) shows similar but different arrangement that also satisfies momentum conservation.
Step 6: Option (c) shows v₁ up and v₂ down. These cancel vertically but provide no horizontal component to match original v.
solution.explanation: Conservation of momentum requires that the vector sum of the final momenta equals the initial momentum. Option (a) shows velocity vectors whose sum can equal the original horizontal velocity.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Three ping-pong balls are electrically charged and are arranged in the plane of the page in an equilateral triangle as shown below. What is the direction of the force acting on the ping-pong ball charged with $Q_3 = -10 \mu C$?

choices.a: Towards the top of the page.
choices.b: Towards the bottom of the page.

questions.diagram_description: Triangular arrangement of three charged ping-pong balls showing Q3 = -10μC at top and Q1 = 5μC, Q2 = 5μC at bottom corners

steps: Step 1: Identify charges: Q₁ = +5μC, Q₂ = +5μC, Q₃ = -10μC
Step 2: Analyze force F₁₃: Q₁ and Q₃ have opposite signs, so attractive force. F₁₃ points from Q₃ toward Q₁ (down and left)
Step 3: Analyze force F₂₃: Q₂ and Q₃ have opposite signs, so attractive force. F₂₃ points from Q₃ toward Q₂ (down and right)
Step 4: Both forces have equal magnitude since |Q₁| = |Q₂| and distances are equal
Step 5: Vector addition: Both forces point downward with equal horizontal components that cancel. Net force points straight down
Step 6: Therefore, the net force on Q₃ is toward the bottom of the page
solution.explanation: The negative charge Q₃ is attracted to both positive charges Q₁ and Q₂. Due to symmetry, the horizontal components cancel and the net force points downward.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: In the circuit shown below, the resistance $R_1$ is increased. What happens to the magnitude of the potential difference across $R_1$?
questions.diagram_description: Circuit diagram showing resistors R1 and R2 with a voltage source V and capacitor

steps: Step 1: Identify circuit configuration - R1 and R2 are in series with voltage source V
Step 2: Apply Ohm's law for series circuit: $I = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2}$
Step 3: Voltage across R1 is: $V_{R1} = I \cdot R_1 = \frac{V \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$
Step 4: When R1 increases, analyze the fraction $\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$
Step 5: As R1 increases, both numerator and denominator increase, but the rate of change shows the fraction approaches 1, so V_R1 increases
solution.explanation: When R1 increases in a series circuit, the voltage across R1 increases because it takes a larger fraction of the total voltage drop, even though the current decreases.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two identical loudspeakers, placed close to each other, are supplied with the same sinusoidal voltage. One can imagine a pattern around the loudspeakers with alternating areas of increased and decreased sound intensity. Which of the actions below will not change the positions of these areas?

choices.b: Changing the amplitude of the voltage.
choices.d: Replacing the air in the room with a gas of a different density.

steps: Step 1: The interference pattern from two identical speakers depends on the wavelength of the sound waves and the geometry of the setup.
Step 2: The positions of constructive and destructive interference areas depend on the path difference between waves from the two speakers being integer multiples of wavelengths.

Step 3: Analyze each option: a) Moving speakers changes geometry and path differences - changes pattern positions. b) Changing amplitude only affects intensity, not wavelength or path differences - pattern positions unchanged. c) Changing frequency changes wavelength (λ = v/f), altering the interference pattern positions. d) Changing gas density changes wave speed, which changes wavelength for same frequency, altering pattern positions.
Step 4: Only changing amplitude affects the strength of the interference but not the spatial positions of the interference pattern.
solution.explanation: Changing the amplitude only affects the intensity of the sound but does not change the wavelength or the geometric relationships that determine where constructive and destructive interference occur.

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Two artificial satellites, named Argo and Navis, have circular orbits of radii $R$ and $2R$, respectively, about the same planet. The orbital speed of Argo is $v$. What is the orbital speed of Navis?

steps: Step 1: For circular orbits, gravitational force provides centripetal force: $\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$
Step 2: Solving for orbital velocity: $v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}$
Step 3: This shows that orbital velocity is inversely proportional to the square root of orbital radius: $v \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$
Step 4: For Argo at radius R: $v_{Argo} = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{R}} = v$
Step 5: For Navis at radius 2R: $v_{Navis} = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{2R}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{GM}{R}} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{2}}$
solution.explanation: Since orbital velocity is inversely proportional to the square root of orbital radius, doubling the radius reduces the velocity by a factor of √2

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: When someone drags their fingernails across a chalkboard, a terrible high-pitched sound is produced due to small bumps in the chalkboard. Assume these bumps are uniformly spaced by 0.5 mm. Audiologists have determined that humans find sounds in the range of 2 ~ 4 kHz to be very annoying. An evil teacher wants to produce the longest duration continuous sound in this range by dragging her fingernails across the chalkboard. At what speed should she drag her nails to accomplish this?

steps: Step 1: Identify that the bumps create periodic motion with wavelength λ = 0.5 mm = 0.0005 m
Step 2: Use the wave equation f = v/λ where v is the dragging speed and f is the frequency
Step 3: For longest duration sound in the annoying range (2-4 kHz), use the lowest frequency f = 2000 Hz
Step 4: Solve for speed: v = f × λ = 2000 Hz × 0.0005 m = 1.00 m/s
Step 5: Verify this produces the lowest frequency in the annoying range, giving the longest duration continuous sound
solution.explanation: To produce the longest duration sound in the annoying frequency range, use the lowest frequency (2 kHz) which requires a speed of 1.00 m/s

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: The Hall Effect occurs when both a current and magnetic field are present and perpendicular to each other in a solid. The result is the generation of an electric field and a corresponding potential difference (the Hall voltage) across the width of the solid. Suppose a two-dimensional rectangular material carries a current of 0.5 A in the positive x direction and is penetrated by a magnetic field of 1.4 mT in the negative z direction. The number of mobile charges per unit area of the material is $0.2\mu C/m^2$. What is the magnitude of the Hall voltage, and the direction of the generated electric field? (Assume a right-handed coordinate system)

steps: Step 1: Identify given values - Current I = 0.5 A (+x direction), B = 1.4 mT (-z direction), charge density n·q = 0.2 μC/m²
Step 2: Apply right-hand rule for Lorentz force. Current in +x, B in -z, so force on positive charges is in +y direction
Step 3: Charge accumulation creates electric field opposing the magnetic force, so E field points in -y direction
Step 4: For a 2D material, the Hall electric field is E_H = IB/(nq·t) where t is thickness. For 2D case, use surface charge density
Step 6: The problem asks for voltage magnitude and field direction. Field is in -y direction with magnitude 3.5 kV/m
solution.explanation: The Hall electric field magnitude is 3.5 kV/m in the negative y direction due to charge separation from the Lorentz force

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: $^{214}$Po atoms have a mass of $3.55 \times 10^{-22}$ g and decay into $^{210}$Pb with a half-life of 160 μs. A detector encompassing 1g of $^{214}$Po counts the number of $^{210}$Pb daughters produced. An experimentalist rigs an oscillator so that the frequency of electromagnetic radiation it emits matches the frequency of $^{210}$Pb counts measured by detector. After 8 ms, what type of electromagnetic radiation is produced by the oscillator?

steps: Step 1: Calculate initial number of atoms: $N_0 = \frac{1 \text{ g}}{3.55 \times 10^{-22} \text{ g}} = 2.82 \times 10^{21}$ atoms
Step 2: Find decay constant: $\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{160 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 4.33 \times 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$
Step 3: Calculate number of atoms remaining after 8 ms: $N(8 \times 10^{-3}) = N_0 e^{-4.33 \times 10^3 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}} = N_0 e^{-34.6} \approx 0$
Step 4: Calculate decay rate (frequency) at t = 8 ms: Since almost all atoms have decayed, the frequency is very low
Step 5: The frequency of decay matches the oscillator frequency, which corresponds to radar frequencies (low frequency electromagnetic radiation)
solution.explanation: After 8 ms (50 half-lives), virtually all Po-214 atoms have decayed, leaving a very low decay rate corresponding to radar frequency electromagnetic radiation

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: To submerge a block of wood which is less dense than water, one needs to exert a force downward which does a positive amount of work on the block. Which of the following is correct for this situation to occur?

choices.a: The work done by the external force is stored as potential energy in the block.
choices.b: The block is moving downward, therefore its potential energy is decreasing, thus the work done by the external force is all converted to heat, due to friction.
choices.c: The work done by external force is all stored as kinetic energy in the block.
choices.d: The potential energy of the water is increased while the potential energy of the block is decreased.
choices.e: The total energy of the block is conserved.
choices.f: The total energy of the block and water is conserved.

steps: Step 1: Analyze forces on the submerged wood block. Since wood is less dense than water, buoyant force exceeds weight.
Step 2: For the block to move downward at constant velocity (quasi-static process), external force must overcome net upward force.
Step 3: Apply energy conservation. External work goes into: (1) increasing gravitational PE of displaced water, (2) decreasing gravitational PE of block, (3) any kinetic energy changes.
Step 4: When wood is pushed down, it displaces water upward. The displaced water gains gravitational potential energy.
Step 5: The block loses gravitational potential energy as it moves down, but the system (block + water) gains net potential energy.
Step 6: In the absence of friction and assuming slow motion (negligible KE), total energy of the block-water system is conserved.
solution.explanation: The external work done increases the potential energy of the displaced water more than it decreases the potential energy of the block, but total energy of the block-water system remains conserved (assuming no friction).

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: In 1929, Edwin Hubble discovered that the universe is expanding. He observed that galaxies far away from us are moving away at a speed that is proportional to their distance from us (you can assume the constant of proportionality is time-independent). For a galaxy that obeys Hubble's law, which of the following can be the graph of distance (from Earth) versus time? For each plot, t = 0 corresponds to the present.

choices.b: Graph showing straight line through origin

questions.diagram_description: Four graphs labeled a), b), c), d) showing different curve relationships, likely distance vs time plots for Hubble's law analysis

steps: Step 1: Apply Hubble's Law: velocity is proportional to distance, so v = H₀d where H₀ is Hubble's constant
Step 2: Since velocity is the time derivative of distance: v = dd/dt
Step 3: Combining these: dd/dt = H₀d
Step 4: This is a first-order differential equation with solution d(t) = d₀e^(H₀t)
Step 5: Since t = 0 corresponds to present time, and we want distance vs time going backward (when universe was smaller), the graph should show exponential growth from past to present
solution.explanation: Hubble's law leads to exponential expansion, so distance vs time follows an exponential curve

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: Which of the following is closest to the thickness of a piece of paper?

steps: Step 1: Consider everyday experience with paper thickness
Step 2: A typical sheet of copy/printer paper is about 0.1 mm thick
Step 3: Convert 0.1 mm to meters: 0.1 mm = 0.1 × 10^-3 m = 1 × 10^-4 m
Step 4: Compare with given options - 10^-4 m matches our estimate
Step 5: Verify reasonableness: 10^-3 m = 1 mm (too thick), 10^-5 m = 0.01 mm (too thin)
solution.explanation: A typical piece of paper is approximately 0.1 mm thick, which equals 10^-4 m

questions.subject: physics

questions.text: The paper-folding theorem states that in order to fold something in half, it's length must be at least π times its thickness. How many times can you fold a standard sheet of printing paper (0.216 m × 0.279 m) if you always fold from the middle of the longer edge?
